saas.sac_description: Information technology (IT) consulting and support services
rules: 18% GST on all SaaS invoices — both B2B and B2C customers in India
B2B customers can claim ITC (input tax credit) on your GST — it's not their real cost
B2C customers bear the full 18% — consider whether to include in listed price or add-on
Exports of SaaS (foreign customers billed in USD) are ZERO-rated — claim ITC refund on inputs
Place of Supply for SaaS = customer's location. For foreign customers: export = 0% GST
If annual turnover > ₹20 lakh (₹10 lakh in special states), GST registration mandatory
File GSTR-1 by 11th of each month (outward supplies) and GSTR-3B by 20th

examples.refund_available: Yes — claim ITC refund on inputs used to serve this client

common_mistakes: Not registering for GST when crossing ₹20 lakh threshold — leads to penalties
Not filing GSTR-9 annual return — required for registered taxpayers
Treating B2B and B2C invoices the same (different Place of Supply rules)
Not claiming GST ITC on cloud hosting costs (AWS/Azure) — you're entitled to ITC
Not converting USD invoices to INR using correct RBI rate for GST filing

rules: GST rate depends on your product category — check HSN code carefully
ITC available on raw materials, packaging, and inputs at every stage
For own-website D2C: collect GST from customer, file returns
For marketplace sales (Amazon/Flipkart): marketplace collects and remits TCS (1%); you still file GSTR
Return/refund: GST refund to customer if full refund; partial refund = partial GST credit note
COD (Cash on Delivery) orders: GST applies on invoice date, not payment receipt

product_gst_rates.examples: Biscuits, instant noodles, namkeen, breakfast cereals

common_mistakes: Wrong HSN code classification — leads to tax notices and differential demand
Not accounting for reverse logistics GST — when goods return, credit note required
Marketplace TCS (1%) not reconciled in GSTR-2A — causes mismatches
Selling across state lines without understanding state-wise GST registration (mandatory for each state above threshold)

rules: Marketplace platforms are 'Deemed Suppliers' for GST on e-commerce under Section 9(5) — you collect and remit GST on behalf of sellers for specific services
Your platform's commission/take-rate income: 18% GST
As a marketplace, you are responsible for TCS (Tax Collected at Source): collect 1% GST TCS from sellers on net value of sales made through your platform
File GSTR-8 monthly to report TCS collected
Reverse Charge Mechanism (RCM): for certain services you purchase (from unregistered vendors), you must pay GST and claim ITC
For marketplaces: Aggregator of services (cab aggregator, food delivery) must collect 18% GST on value of services — not just commission

examples.scenario: Seller sells ₹1,00,000 of goods through your platform

common_mistakes: Not filing GSTR-8 (TCS return) — mandatory for all e-commerce operators
Confusing commission GST with TCS — they are separate obligations
Not understanding deemed supplier rules for service aggregators (Section 9(5))
itc_claimable_expenses: Payment gateway fees (18% GST on MDR)

rules: Full ITC chain: GST on raw materials → manufacturing inputs → finished goods → sale. ITC flows through entire chain
Job work (outsourcing manufacturing to third party): GST applicable on job work charges at 12% (for most goods)
Inverted duty structure: if input GST rate > output rate, claim refund (common in textiles, some food processing)
Export of goods: Zero-rated. File refund for accumulated ITC under Rule 89
Annual turnover > ₹1.5 crore: mandatory GSTR-1 + GSTR-3B filing
Composition scheme: Small manufacturers with turnover < ₹1.5 crore can pay flat 1% GST (but can't claim ITC)
common_mistakes: Not tracking input-output ratio for ITC claims — tax authorities scrutinize this
Job work challan not issued for goods sent to contract manufacturer
Not claiming refund when inverted duty structure applies

rules: Export of services is zero-rated under IGST — no GST on invoices to foreign customers
Two options: (a) Pay IGST and claim refund, or (b) Export under LUT (Letter of Undertaking) — no payment, direct refund
LUT filing: File LUT on GST portal annually before starting exports. Highly recommended — avoids cash flow issues
Foreign currency receipts must come through banking channels (SWIFT/FEMA compliant) to qualify as exports
Claim refund of ITC accumulated on inputs used to provide export services (within 2 years)
FIRC (Foreign Inward Remittance Certificate) from bank is critical proof of export service payment

examples.scenario: US startup pays $10,000/month for your SaaS
examples.proof_needed: Invoice + FIRC from bank + GSTIN details
examples.itc_refund: Claim refund for ITC on expenses (cloud, salaries excluded, but hosting yes)

common_mistakes: Not filing LUT — leads to paying IGST upfront (cash flow hit) and then claiming refund
Not getting FIRC from bank for every USD receipt — required for export documentation
Billing in USD but customer is Indian subsidiary of foreign company — this is NOT export of services
Mixing domestic and export billing on same GST invoice — keep separate invoice series

compliance_calendar.Refund application: Monthly or quarterly via Form RFD-01
